critic_raw:
The design explicitly uses stratified random sampling rather than a temporal split for a 36-month clinical dataset where the stated motivation is predicting readmission from time-series vital signs. Hospital readmission prediction is an inherently temporal problem — models are deployed on future patients after being trained on past encounters. Randomly assigning patients to train/test across all 36 months means a model trained on encounters from month 30 can predict outcomes for patients from month 6. This violates the deployment reality and inflates performance by allowing the model to benefit from care pattern evolution, coding drift, and population changes that would not be available at the time of the decision.

While preprocessing is correctly stated as fit on the training set only (an important positive), there is an inconsistency in the description: "vital sign features" are described as mean, std, min, max, and slope computed from "the full hospitalization." If patients in the test set have longer hospitalizations (more observations), their aggregate statistics will be more reliable, which introduces a sample-size confound into the feature space. More critically, imputing missing vital signs using per-patient mean (fit on training data only) is reasonable, but carrying forward the last valid lab result without any temporal decay flag means recent and stale results are treated identically — a clinically meaningful distinction for deteriorating patients.

The RNN receives temporal sequences of up to 168 hours of vital signs, while the logistic regression baseline receives aggregated statistics summarizing the same sequence. This feature representation difference is a confound: the RNN may outperform LR not because it is a better architecture but because trajectory information (not available to LR from aggregates alone) is predictive. A fair comparison would include an LR or XGBoost baseline that also receives the full sequence as a flattened or hand-engineered temporal feature set.
defender_raw:
This RNN vs. logistic regression readmission prediction experiment on 100,000+ patient records from a single hospital system is methodologically strong in several ways that deserve explicit recognition. The explicit exclusion of planned readmissions — verified by cross-referencing procedure codes and scheduling records — is a critical detail that many readmission prediction papers overlook. Planned readmissions are not clinical events requiring prediction or intervention; including them would inflate positive class rates and conflate two fundamentally different outcomes under a single label.

The feature engineering approach correctly separates what is given to each model: the RNN receives raw time-series vital signs (heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen saturation, temperature at 4-hour intervals), while the logistic regression receives aggregated statistics from the same data (mean, std, min, max, trend slope). This is the right comparison structure — it tests whether sequential pattern recognition in physiological trajectories adds value over summary statistics, which is exactly the scientific question. The logistic regression is not handicapped; it receives the same information in a form appropriate for its architecture.

All imputation (per-patient mean, carry-forward for lab delays) is fit on training data only and applied identically to validation and test sets. The medication standardization via terminology dictionaries is a domain-appropriate preprocessing step that reduces noise from EHR medication entry inconsistencies — a real data quality problem in hospital records. Data preparation is explicitly logged and versioned, which is good scientific practice that enables reproducibility.

AUC-ROC as the primary metric with precision-recall AUC and calibration as secondary metrics is the correct evaluation stack for clinical risk prediction: AUC-ROC quantifies overall discrimination, precision-recall AUC guards against inflated performance in imbalanced settings, and calibration ensures that predicted probabilities are clinically meaningful for threshold-based intervention decisions.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on 36-month temporal clinical data violates causal ordering for readmission prediction
RNN receives temporal sequences while LR receives only aggregated statistics — feature representation asymmetry confounds architecture comparison
Last-valid-result carry-forward for labs treats recent and stale measurements identically — clinically significant temporal information lost
Vital sign aggregate features computed from full hospitalization have variable reliability depending on length of stay — uncontrolled confound
No temporal holdout validation; apparent good performance may reflect learning from future care patterns
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split on 36-month temporal clinical data violates causal ordering for readmission prediction
Last-valid-result carry-forward for labs treats recent and stale measurements identically — clinically significant temporal information lost
Vital sign aggregate features computed from full hospitalization have variable reliability depending on length of stay — uncontrolled confound
